tools.name: find_package
tools.description: Install support for a capability you do NOT have yet: search Hackage for the package to add. Describe the task in words ("linear regression", "parse JSON", "read csv") and get ranked packages, each with the exact `-- cabal:` build-depends line to drop into a cell. THIS is the tool when the answer is 'add a dependency' / 'which package / what do I install'. FIRST step for any new capability; once the package is installed, switch to find_function to find the call inside it.
tools.rationale: Leads with the install/add-a-dependency intent and the trigger phrases ('which package', 'what do I install') so a JSON/regression 'I need to add X' prompt routes here instead of bailing to no tool.

tools.name: find_function
tools.description: Search the LIVE installed packages for a function by NAME or KEYWORD, or list everything a module EXPORTS by passing its module name ("DataFrame", "Granite.Svg") — returns module + signature for each hit. Use for 'what's the call for X', 'which module is Y in', or 'show me everything module Z exports'. This queries the real installed API right now. Differences: to search by a TYPE signature use find_by_type; for a value YOU defined use list_bindings; for the static cheat-sheet card with no session use api_reference; for prose docs use describe_function.
tools.rationale: find_function_2 ('everything Granite.Svg exports') lost 3/3 to api_reference; now the module-exports intent and the LIVE-vs-static-card contrast are explicit in the lead, and api_reference no longer fronts the same module names.

tools.name: find_by_type
tools.description: Find an installed function whose TYPE matches a goal type you supply, when you know the shape but not the name. Pass a type like "[Int] -> Int" or a hole "_ :: Ord a => [a] -> [a]". Differs from find_function, which searches by name/keyword, not by type. (search_by_type does the same Hoogle-by-type search; either is fine.)
tools.rationale: Already 100%; only added a note that search_by_type is an accepted equivalent, matching the battery's shared accept set, without disturbing routing.

tools.name: list_bindings
tools.description: List the values, functions, and types you have ALREADY defined in this notebook session, each with its type — the things currently in scope from your earlier cells. Use BEFORE writing a new cell to reuse an existing binding instead of recomputing it. Note: find_function searches library APIs, not your own bindings; resolve_name maps a name back to the cell that defines it.

tools.name: check_type
tools.description: What is the type of this expression? Type-check an expression WITHOUT running it and get its type — pass an expression like "map fst . filter snd" or "map fst". Also returns the kind or definition of a type or class you name (e.g. "Traversable"). Use whenever you have a concrete expression or a known name and want its type/kind, no execution. To discover a name you do NOT know, use find_function or find_by_type.
tools.rationale: check_type_1 ('type of map fst . filter snd') went null 3/3 because the lead emphasised 'a type/class you ALREADY know'; now it opens with the expression-type case verbatim so the expression intent matches first.

tools.name: find_example_cell
tools.description: Get a whole ready-made, runnable example CELL to copy-paste and adapt for a task ("read csv", "typed column") — returns full cell source you can tweak, not docs and not a single function. Use when you want a complete worked snippet to paste. Differs from find_function/find_package, which name a single function or package rather than handing you a pasteable cell.
tools.rationale: find_example_cell_1 ('ready-made cell I can paste and tweak') leaked to find_function; the lead now stresses 'whole runnable CELL to copy-paste' vs 'a single function' to win the paste-a-snippet intent.

tools.name: peek_data
tools.description: Inspect a CSV/TSV file before loading it: returns the delimiter, whether row one is a header, the first N rows, and a guessed type per column. Use to shape a DataFrame read instead of guessing the schema.

tools.name: list_cells
tools.description: Get the notebook's structure: every cell's id, type, language, error flag, and first line. Start HERE to see the lay of the land or which cells are flagged broken. This only LISTS cells — it does not read full source (use read_cell / export_notebook) and does not change anything (to edit a cell use edit_cell, to suggest a change for approval use suggest_cell_edit).
tools.rationale: list_cells was stealing edit intents (replace_cell_source/propose_edit) on the weak model's 'look first' reflex; added an explicit 'this only lists, to change a cell use edit_cell/suggest_cell_edit' steer.

tools.name: read_cell
tools.description: Read ONE cell's full source AND its outputs/errors, by id. Use after list_cells to inspect a specific cell's contents. If you only need the result, use read_cell_output. This is read-only — to change/fix a cell use edit_cell (or suggest_cell_edit for a user-owned cell).
tools.rationale: read_cell was stealing edit intents; the closing steer tells the model that fixing a cell is edit_cell, not reading it first.

tools.name: read_cell_output
tools.description: Read only a cell's outputs and error (not its source), by id. Use to check what a cell produced or why it failed; use read_cell when you also need the source.

tools.name: find_cells_by_content
tools.description: Find cells whose source contains a substring; returns matching cell ids with context. Use to locate where something is defined or used.

tools.name: export_notebook
tools.description: Return every cell's id, position, type, language, and source in one call — a whole-notebook snapshot in a single request instead of many read_cell calls.

tools.name: insert_cell
tools.description: Append a NEW cell at the end of the notebook and run it. Use to add code or prose. Cannot be used while another cell has an unresolved error — fix that cell (edit_cell) or remove it (delete_cell) first. To change an EXISTING cell use edit_cell.
tools.rationale: Updated the cross-references from the old name replace_cell_source to edit_cell so the steer stays valid after the rename.

tools.name: edit_cell
tools.description: Edit, rewrite, or fix an EXISTING cell and re-run it: replace its entire source with the new version you provide (full replacement, not a diff), applied immediately. This is the tool for 'rewrite this cell', 'fix the error in cell N', 'swap out / change the contents of cell N and run it again' — for cells you created or that are yours to apply directly. Do NOT call read_cell or list_cells first; this changes the cell itself. To ADD a new cell use insert_cell. For a cell the USER authored, where the change must be reviewed before it runs, use suggest_cell_edit instead.
tools.rationale: Was replace_cell_source at 0% — both prompts ('rewrite its whole body', 'swap out the entire contents') went to read_cell/list_cells. Renamed to the verb users use (edit/rewrite/fix), led with imperatives, and added 'do NOT read_cell/list_cells first; this changes the cell' to kill the look-first reflex; author axis (apply now vs review) points at suggest_cell_edit.

tools.name: delete_cell
tools.description: Remove a cell by id. Use to delete a cell you cannot fix in place — e.g. a failing cell blocking insert_cell — or an abandoned/duplicate cell. Prefer edit_cell to fix a cell rather than deleting and re-adding.
tools.rationale: Updated cross-reference to the renamed edit_cell.

tools.name: suggest_cell_edit
tools.description: Suggest an edit to a cell the USER authored, held for their approval — the change is NOT applied or run until the user accepts it. This is the tool for 'don't just apply it, suggest a revision and let them approve', 'draft a change but hold it for review' on a user-owned cell. Like edit_cell it rewrites a cell's source, but it PROPOSES rather than applies. For cells you created yourself (apply directly, no approval), use edit_cell instead.
tools.rationale: Was propose_edit at 0% — both prompts went to list_cells/read_cell. Renamed to keep 'suggest'+'edit' (the user's verbs) while the 'suggest_' prefix and 'held for approval, not applied' lead encode the review axis that separates it from edit_cell.

tools.name: execute_cell
tools.description: Run an existing cell by id, as-is, and wait for its result. Use to re-run a cell you have NOT changed (e.g. to pick up a new upstream binding). To change a cell's source use edit_cell; to run throwaway code without touching the notebook use scratchpad.
tools.rationale: Updated cross-reference to the renamed edit_cell; clarified 'as-is / not changed' so it stays distinct from edit_cell.

tools.name: scratchpad
tools.description: Run a SELF-CONTAINED snippet in an isolated session that CANNOT see the notebook's cells or bindings (same packages, separate state). Use for a throwaway experiment. To run something that uses notebook bindings, put it in a cell instead.

tools.name: kernel_status
tools.description: Report the kernel's state (cold | idle | executing | building) without blocking, even while a cell is running. Use to tell busy from wedged. To wait for a running cell use await_idle; to recover a wedged kernel use kernel_restart.

tools.name: await_idle
tools.description: Block until the running cell finishes (bounded ~45s), then return the kernel status. Use after kicking off a long cell to wait for it to settle before doing anything else. Cheaper than polling kernel_status in a loop.
tools.rationale: Its only miss was an HTTP 500 infra error, not a misroute; kept semantics intact with a tiny lead tightening only.

tools.name: interrupt
tools.description: Abort the cell the kernel is currently running. No-op if idle. Try this first when a cell is stuck; if the kernel ignores it, use kernel_restart.

tools.name: kernel_restart
tools.description: Hard-reset the kernel: force-kill the process (even a wedged one) and respawn it clean, reusing installed packages without rebuilding and without re-running cells. Use to recover a kernel that interrupt could not free.
tools.rationale: Its only miss was an HTTP 500 infra error, not a misroute; left semantics unchanged apart from a one-word grammar fix.

tools.name: api_reference
tools.description: Fetch the curated, static cheat-sheet signature card for the dataframe and granite plotting APIs. Works with NO running session — use it specifically when no cell has run yet, or when you want the maintained reference card rather than a live lookup. Pass a module substring (or omit for the whole card). To list what a module ACTUALLY exports in the live installed session, use find_function instead.

module.description: Module substring, or empty for all.

tools.rationale: This tool was stealing find_function_2's 'show me everything Granite.Svg exports' 3/3; the lead no longer fronts specific module names and now scopes itself to 'static cheat-sheet, no session', explicitly handing live module-exports queries to find_function. Both api_reference battery intents still match (they say 'curated card / cheat sheet / no running session').

tools.name: describe_function
tools.description: Explain in PROSE what an installed function or type DOES — its haddock documentation / behaviour, beyond just the type. Use when you know the name and want to understand how it behaves ("explain what pivot does", "walk me through interpolate's docs"). Differs from check_type (which gives only the type), from locate_import (which gives the import line / module for a name), and from find_function (which finds a name you don't know).
tools.rationale: describe_function_1 ('explain in prose what pivot does') lost 1/3 to locate_import; the lead now stresses PROSE/behaviour/documentation and explicitly contrasts the import-line role of locate_import.

tools.name: locate_import
tools.description: Given a function/type/operator NAME you already know, return which module exports it and the exact import line to paste. This is an authoritative reverse name->module lookup, and unlike a keyword search it works even when the name is not yet imported into the session. Use it the moment you write a name and aren't sure of its module or package. Differs from describe_function (prose docs / behaviour, not an import line), from find_function (keyword/fuzzy discovery of an unknown name), and from check_type (the type, not the module).

name.description: The exact identifier you want to import, e.g. "columnAsList", "scatter", "fromList", "(.|.)". Unqualified; no module prefix.

tools.rationale: Replaced the phantom cross-references (find_module_capabilities, ghci_query op=info) with the real tool names, and added a contrast against describe_function so it stops absorbing the 'explain what X does' prose intent it stole on one seed.

tools.name: search_by_type
tools.description: Find functions whose SIGNATURE matches a type you want, e.g. "Text -> DataFrame" or "FilePath -> IO DataFrame" — Hoogle-by-type search ranked by how well the arrow shape fits. Pass a type signature (containing `->`) when you know the input and output types but not the function name; use it for 'I have an X, I need a Y' lookups. (find_by_type does the same type-shape search; either is fine. For keyword/name search use find_function.)

signature.description: A type signature containing `->`, e.g. "FilePath -> IO DataFrame", "[a] -> Int", "Double -> Picture". Concrete or polymorphic types both work.

tools.rationale: Already 100%; trimmed the phantom find_module_capabilities reference and noted find_by_type as an accepted equivalent, matching the shared accept set.

tools.name: show_usage
tools.description: Show a short runnable snippet that CALLS a specific named function, so you can see argument order and a real invocation before writing your own. Pass one function name and get a concrete usage line. Differs from find_example_cell (which searches curated whole-cell idioms by task keyword, not by function name) and from describe_function (which returns prose/signature, never a call site). Use it after you have the signature but are unsure how the arguments go together.

name.description: The exact function name to show a call example for, e.g. "groupBy", "readCsv", "lineGraph". Unqualified.

tools.rationale: Already 100%; replaced the phantom names (find_examples, ghci_query op=doc) with the real tool names find_example_cell and describe_function.

tools.name: list_errors
tools.description: List every cell that currently has an error, each with its cell id, the error line, and the actual error message, in one call. Use this to see the whole notebook's broken state at a glance; unlike list_cells (which only flags hasError as a boolean), this returns the messages so you don't have to call read_cell_output on each failing cell. Returns an empty list when nothing is broken.
tools.rationale: Already 100%; left intact.

tools.name: resolve_name
tools.description: Find which cell DEFINES a given top-level name (a function, type, or value binding) and whether it is in scope anywhere in the notebook. Use when you have a name and need its origin cell. Unlike find_cells_by_content (raw substring match over all sources), this returns the single cell that canonically defines the name plus the cell ids that USE it; unlike list_bindings (live session variables with types but no cell provenance), this maps the name back to its notebook cell. Reports not-defined when no cell binds it.

name.description: The exact top-level identifier to resolve, e.g. "df", "trainModel", "MyRecord".

tools.rationale: Already 100%; only updated the contrast to name list_bindings (its real sibling) rather than the old ghci_query phrasing.

tools.name: trace_cell_deps
tools.description: Show the reactive dependency edges of one cell: which cells it depends on (the names it consumes and the cells that define them) and which cells depend on it (its transitive downstream blast radius). Use this before editing or deleting a cell to learn what will re-run or break — list_cells gives only a flat positional list and await_idle only waits for a cascade already started, whereas this predicts the cascade. Pass direction for just upstream, just downstream, or both.

cell_id.description: The cell whose dependency edges to trace.

direction.description: upstream = cells this one needs; downstream = cells that re-run if this one changes; both = both sets. Default both.

tools.rationale: Already 100%; left intact.
